title: Find single number
description: Find the element that appears once when others appear twice

examples.explanation: Step 1: Start with array [2, 2, 1]. Outer loop i=0, element=2. Inner loop j=0 to 2: j=0 (skip self), j=1 element=2 matches (found duplicate), break inner loop. Step 2: Outer loop i=1, element=2. Inner loop j=0 to 2: j=0 element=2 matches (found duplicate), break inner loop. Step 3: Outer loop i=2, element=1. Inner loop j=0 to 2: j=0 element=2 no match, j=1 element=2 no match, j=2 (skip self). Complete inner loop without finding duplicate. Return 1.

Step 1: Array [4, 1, 2, 1, 2]. Outer loop i=0, element=4. Inner loop j=0 to 4: j=0 skip, j=1 element=1 no match, j=2 element=2 no match, j=3 element=1 no match, j=4 element=2 no match. No duplicate found, return 4 immediately. (Note: algorithm can stop early once single number is found, but full trace shows all comparisons if continued).

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each element, check if it appears again in the array by scanning the entire list. Use this when simplicity is prioritized over efficiency, but it's inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Single Number with Brute Force O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete solution using nested loops to find the element that appears exactly once when all others appear twice.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the 'Find Single Number' problem by comparing each element with every other element in the array to determine if it appears more than once. The intuition is straightforward: since all elements except one appear exactly twice, the unique element will not have a matching pair when we search through the entire array. We iterate through each element using an outer loop, and for each element, we use an inner loop to scan the entire array looking for a duplicate. If we find a duplicate (excluding the element comparing with itself), we know this element appears twice. If we complete the inner loop without finding any duplicate, we've found the single number. This approach prioritizes simplicity over efficiency, making it easy to understand and implement, but it becomes impractical for large arrays due to its O(n²) time complexity. The space complexity is O(1) since we only use a few variables for iteration and comparison. This connects directly to the problem statement by providing a complete, albeit inefficient, solution. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like arrays with one element, incorrectly comparing an element with itself (which would always find a 'duplicate'), or forgetting to break out of loops once the single number is found. The real code pattern involves nested for loops with careful index management to avoid self-comparison.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with array [2, 2, 1]. Outer loop i=0, element=2. Inner loop j=0 to 2: j=0 (skip self), j=1 element=2 matches (found duplicate), break inner loop. Step 2: Outer loop i=1, element=2. Inner loop j=0 to 2: j=0 element=2 matches (found duplicate), break inner loop. Step 3: Outer loop i=2, element=1. Inner loop j=0 to 2: j=0 element=2 no match, j=1 element=2 no match, j=2 (skip self). Complete inner loop without finding duplicate. Return 1.

Step 1: Array [4, 1, 2, 1, 2]. Outer loop i=0, element=4. Inner loop j=0 to 4: j=0 skip, j=1 element=1 no match, j=2 element=2 no match, j=3 element=1 no match, j=4 element=2 no match. No duplicate found, return 4 immediately. (Note: algorithm can stop early once single number is found, but full trace shows all comparisons if continued).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check If Element Appears Twice in Array
ladders.desc: Helper function that scans an array to determine if a given target element appears more than once.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach, responsible for checking whether a specific element appears multiple times in the array. The intuition is that in the 'Find Single Number' problem, we need to identify elements that have duplicates; this function performs that check for one element at a time. The algorithm takes an array and a target element as input, then iterates through the array counting how many times the target appears. If the count is exactly 1 (meaning only the element itself was found), it returns false (no duplicate); if count is 2 or more, it returns true (has duplicate). In practice, we can optimize by stopping early once we find a second occurrence. Time complexity is O(n) for scanning the array once, and space complexity is O(1) using only a counter variable. This connects to the full problem (L0) by being called for each element in the outer loop. Common mistakes include counting the element itself as a duplicate (must handle self-comparison carefully), or not considering that elements might appear more than twice (though problem states exactly twice for duplicates). The real code pattern involves a simple for loop with a counter and an early exit condition.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize count=0. Step 2: Iterate array: index 0 element=2 matches target, count=1. index 1 element=2 matches target, count=2. Since count>=2, return true (has duplicate).

Step 1: count=0. Step 2: Iterate: index 0 element=4 matches, count=1. index 1 element=1 no match. index 2 element=2 no match. index 3 element=1 no match. index 4 element=2 no match. After loop, count=1, return false (no duplicate).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Nested Loop Comparison for Duplicate Detection
ladders.desc: The central technique of using two loops to compare each element with all others to find uniqueness.
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the nested loop structure that defines the brute force O(n²) approach. The intuition is that to determine if an element is unique, we must check it against every other element in the array; this requires an outer loop to select each element and an inner loop to perform the comparisons. The algorithm works by iterating through each index i in the array (outer loop), and for each i, iterating through all indices j (inner loop) to compare array[i] with array[j]. If a match is found where i ≠ j, we know array[i] has a duplicate and is not the single number. If no match is found after checking all j, array[i] is the single number. Time complexity is O(n²) because for n elements, we perform up to n comparisons each, leading to n*n operations. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use loop variables. This connects to L1 (Key Sub-routine) by encapsulating the duplicate check within the loop structure. Common mistakes include comparing an element with itself (i == j), which would incorrectly flag all elements as duplicates, or not breaking out of loops early once the single number is found (though early break doesn't change worst-case time complexity). The real code pattern features nested for loops with an if condition to skip self-comparison and check for equality.

examples.trace: Step 1: Outer i=0 (element=3). Inner j=0 to 2: j=0 skip self, j=1 element=3 matches (duplicate found), break inner. Step 2: Outer i=1 (element=3). Inner j=0 to 2: j=0 element=3 matches (duplicate), break. Step 3: Outer i=2 (element=7). Inner j=0 to 2: j=0 element=3 no match, j=1 element=3 no match, j=2 skip self. No duplicate found, return 7.
Step 1: i=0 (1). j=0 skip, j=1 element=2 no match, j=2 element=1 match (duplicate), break. Step 2: i=1 (2). j=0 element=1 no match, j=1 skip, j=2 element=1 no match. No duplicate, return 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through Array with Index Tracking
ladders.desc: Intermediate concept of using loop indices to access and compare array elements systematically.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the fundamental skill of traversing an array using indices, which is essential for the nested loop comparison in the brute force approach. The intuition is that to compare elements, we need a way to access each element and its position; array indices provide this reference. The algorithm involves setting up a loop (e.g., for or while) that iterates from 0 to n-1, where n is the array length, using an index variable (like i) to access array[i] at each step. This allows us to examine each element in sequence. For the brute force method, we extend this to two nested loops with indices i and j to access pairs of elements. Time complexity for a single loop is O(n), and space is O(1). This connects to L2 (Core Logic) by providing the mechanism for the outer and inner loops. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., iterating beyond array bounds), confusing zero-based indexing, or not updating the index variable correctly in while loops. The real code pattern is a simple for loop: 'for i in range(len(array)): ... array[i] ...'.

examples.input: Array: [10, 20, 30], print each element with index
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize i=0, array[0]=10, print 'Index 0: 10'. Step 2: i=1, array[1]=20, print 'Index 1: 20'. Step 3: i=2, array[2]=30, print 'Index 2: 30'. Step 4: i=3, loop ends as i >= length 3.

examples.input: Array: [5, 5, 6], compare array[0] with array[1] and array[2]
examples.output: Compare 5 with 5: equal, Compare 5 with 6: not equal
examples.trace: Step 1: Access array[0]=5. Step 2: Compare with array[1]=5: equal. Step 3: Compare with array[2]=6: not equal.

testCases.input: Array: [8, 8, 8], check if all equal by iterating

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Values for Equality
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of checking if two values are equal, used in duplicate detection.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is the simplest action required in the brute force approach: comparing two values to see if they are equal. The intuition is that to find duplicates, we need to identify when two elements have the same value; this comparison is the building block for all duplicate checks. The algorithm involves taking two inputs, say a and b, and using an equality operator (== in most languages) to check if a equals b. If true, they are duplicates; if false, they are different. This operation is constant time O(1) and uses no extra space. It connects to L3 (Building Block) as it's the condition checked inside loops when comparing array[i] and array[j]. Common mistakes include using assignment (=) instead of equality (==), or not considering data types (e.g., comparing integers vs strings). The real code pattern is a simple if statement: 'if a == b: ...'.

examples.trace: Step 1: Take values a=5, b=5. Step 2: Apply equality operator: 5 == 5 evaluates to true. Step 3: Return true.
Step 1: a=10, b=20. Step 2: 10 == 20 evaluates to false. Step 3: Return false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Element Access
ladders.desc: Prerequisite concept of what an array is and how to access its elements.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces arrays, a basic data structure essential for the 'Find Single Number' problem. An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index starting from 0. The intuition is that arrays allow us to store multiple values (like numbers) and retrieve them by position, which is necessary for comparing elements in the brute force approach. The algorithm involves declaring an array (e.g., in Python: arr = [1, 2, 3]), and accessing elements using bracket notation with an index (e.g., arr[0] gives the first element, 1). Key properties include fixed size (in some languages) and O(1) access time for any index. This connects to L4 (Basic Operation) by providing the values to compare. Common mistakes include accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., arr[3] for a 3-element array, which would be invalid), or confusing zero-based indexing (first element is at index 0, not 1). The real code pattern is simple: define an array and use arr[index] to get or set values.

examples.trace: Step 1: Define array arr = [10, 20, 30]. Step 2: Access index 0: arr[0] returns 10. Step 3: Output 10.
Step 1: arr = [5, 6, 7]. Step 2: Index for third element is 2 (zero-based). Step 3: arr[2] returns 7. Step 4: Output 7.

approaches.name: HashMap O(n)
approaches.summary: Count the frequency of each element using a hash map, then find the element with a count of one. This approach is straightforward and works for any input but uses extra space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Single Number Using HashMap
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find the element that appears once when all others appear twice using hash map frequency counting.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that in a collection where every element appears exactly twice except for one element that appears once, we can identify the unique element by tracking how many times each element occurs. A hash map (dictionary) provides an efficient way to store and retrieve these counts. The algorithm works by iterating through the input array once to build a frequency map where each element is a key and its count is the value. Then, we iterate through the map to find the key with a count of 1. This works for any input because it doesn't rely on any special properties of the numbers (like bitwise operations would), making it robust and easy to understand. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty hash map (dictionary). 2. For each element in the input array: a. If the element is not in the map, add it with count 1. b. If it is already in the map, increment its count by 1. 3. After processing all elements, iterate through the map entries. 4. Return the key where the value equals 1. Time complexity is O(n) because we make two passes through the data: one to build the map (O(n) with O(1) average operations per element) and one to find the single count (O(n) in worst case if all elements are unique, but typically O(n) since we stop early when found). Space complexity is O(n) to store the frequency map. Connection to L1: This full solution directly uses the frequency counting subroutine from L1 as its core component. Common mistakes include not handling empty input (should return None or appropriate value), forgetting to check for the single count correctly (e.g., using == instead of checking value), or inefficiently iterating the map multiple times. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def find_single_number(nums): freq = {} for num in nums: if num in freq: freq[num] += 1 else: freq[num] = 1 for num, count in freq.items(): if count == 1: return num return None # or raise error if no single number found

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty freq map {}. Step 2: Process nums: first element 2 -> not in map, add {2:1}. Second element 2 -> in map, increment to {2:2}. Third element 1 -> not in map, add {2:2, 1:1}. Step 3: Iterate map: check 2 -> count=2 (not 1), check 1 -> count=1, return 1.

Step 1: Initialize empty freq map {}. Step 2: Process nums: 4 -> add {4:1}. 1 -> add {4:1, 1:1}. 2 -> add {4:1, 1:1, 2:1}. 1 -> increment to {4:1, 1:2, 2:1}. 2 -> increment to {4:1, 1:2, 2:2}. Step 3: Iterate map: check 4 -> count=1, return 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Count Element Frequencies in Array
ladders.desc: Build a frequency map counting occurrences of each element in an array.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to solve problems involving element counts, we need an efficient way to track how many times each unique element appears. A hash map allows O(1) average time lookups and updates, making it ideal for this task. This subroutine is the core of the full solution because it transforms the input array into a structured representation (frequency counts) that makes finding the single number trivial. The algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty dictionary (hash map). 2. Iterate through each element in the input array. 3. For each element: a. Check if it exists as a key in the dictionary. b. If not, add it with value 1 (first occurrence). c. If yes, increment its value by 1. 4. Return the dictionary. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of elements, as each element is processed once with O(1) average operations. Space complexity is O(k) where k is the number of unique elements, which in worst case is O(n) if all elements are distinct. Connection to L0: This subroutine directly feeds into the full problem by providing the frequency map that L0 then searches for the count of 1. Common mistakes include using a list instead of a dictionary for efficiency (list would be O(n) lookups), not initializing the dictionary properly, or incorrectly handling the increment logic (e.g., setting to 1 instead of incrementing). Real code pattern: def count_frequencies(arr): freq = {} for item in arr: if item in freq: freq[item] += 1 else: freq[item] = 1 return freq

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize {}. Step 2: Process 2 -> not in map, add {2:1}. Step 3: Process 2 -> in map, increment to {2:2}. Step 4: Process 1 -> not in map, add {2:2, 1:1}. Return {2:2, 1:1}.

Step 1: Initialize {}. Step 2: Process 4 -> add {4:1}. Step 3: Process 1 -> add {4:1, 1:1}. Step 4: Process 2 -> add {4:1, 1:1, 2:1}. Step 5: Process 1 -> increment to {4:1, 1:2, 2:1}. Step 6: Process 2 -> increment to {4:1, 1:2, 2:2}. Return {4:1, 1:2, 2:2}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Find Key with Specific Value in Dictionary
ladders.desc: Search through a dictionary to find a key whose value matches a target (e.g., count of 1).
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that once we have a frequency map, we need to extract specific information from it—in this case, the element that appears exactly once. This involves iterating through the key-value pairs and checking if the value equals our target. This is a fundamental dictionary operation that appears in many algorithms. The algorithm: 1. Iterate through each key-value pair in the dictionary. 2. For each pair, check if the value equals the target (e.g., 1). 3. If found, return the key. 4. If no such key is found after checking all pairs, return a sentinel value (like None) or raise an error. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the number of keys in the dictionary, which in worst case is O(n) if all elements are unique. Space complexity is O(1) extra space beyond the input dictionary. Connection to L1: This logic uses the frequency dictionary produced by L1 to find the single number. Common mistakes include not handling the case where no key matches the target (leading to incorrect returns), using inefficient iteration methods, or confusing keys and values. Real code pattern: def find_key_with_value(dictionary, target_value): for key, value in dictionary.items(): if value == target_value: return key return None # or appropriate default

examples.trace: Step 1: Iterate pairs: first pair (2,2) -> value=2, not equal to 1. Step 2: Second pair (1,1) -> value=1, equals target, return key 1.
Step 1: Iterate pairs: (4,1) -> value=1, equals target, return key 4 immediately.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate and Update Dictionary Entries
ladders.desc: Process a collection by updating dictionary values based on keys.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that dictionaries are mutable data structures where we can modify values associated with keys. This building block involves checking if a key exists and then either initializing or updating its value—a pattern used in frequency counting, caching, and many other algorithms. The algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty dictionary. 2. For each item in a list: a. Check if item is a key in the dictionary. b. If not, add it with an initial value (e.g., 1). c. If yes, update its value (e.g., increment by 1). 3. The dictionary now contains updated entries. Time complexity is O(n) for n items, with O(1) average time per dictionary operation. Space complexity is O(k) for k unique keys. Connection to L2: This block is used to build the dictionary that L2 then searches. Common mistakes include using the wrong update logic (e.g., always setting to 1 instead of incrementing), not checking key existence properly, or using inefficient data structures. Real code pattern: def update_dictionary(items): result = {} for item in items: if item in result: result[item] += 1 # Example update else: result[item] = 1 # Example initialization return result

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize {}. Step 2: Process 2 -> not in dict, add {2:1}. Step 3: Process 2 -> in dict, increment to {2:2}. Step 4: Process 1 -> not in dict, add {2:2, 1:1}.

Step 1: Initialize {}. Step 2: Process 4 -> add {4:1}. Step 3: Process 1 -> add {4:1, 1:1}. Step 4: Process 2 -> add {4:1, 1:1, 2:1}. Step 5: Process 1 -> increment to {4:1, 1:2, 2:1}. Step 6: Process 2 -> increment to {4:1, 1:2, 2:2}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check and Modify Dictionary Key-Value Pairs
ladders.desc: Perform fundamental dictionary operations: check if key exists, add new key-value pair, update existing value.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that dictionaries store data as key-value pairs, and we often need to interact with them by checking for keys and modifying values. This operation is foundational because it's used in counting, mapping, and caching scenarios. The algorithm for a single item: 1. Given a dictionary and a key. 2. Check if the key exists in the dictionary (using 'in' operator or get method). 3. If it does not exist, add the key with an initial value (e.g., 1). 4. If it exists, update its value (e.g., increment by 1). Time complexity is O(1) average for these operations due to hash table implementation. Space complexity may increase by O(1) if a new key is added. Connection to L3: This operation is repeated for each item in L3 to build the dictionary. Common mistakes include using incorrect syntax for dictionary access, not handling missing keys properly (leading to KeyError), or confusing keys and values. Real code pattern: def handle_dictionary_entry(dictionary, key): if key in dictionary: dictionary[key] += 1 # Example update else: dictionary[key] = 1 # Example addition

examples.trace: Step 1: Check if key 2 is in dictionary -> no. Step 2: Add key 2 with value 1. Result: {2:1}.
Step 1: Check if key 2 is in dictionary -> yes. Step 2: Update value from 1 to 2. Result: {2:2}.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Dictionary (Hash Map) Basics
ladders.desc: Understand what a dictionary is, how to create one, and perform basic operations like adding and accessing key-value pairs.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a dictionary (also called a hash map) is a data structure that stores data as pairs of keys and values, where each key is unique and maps to a value. It's like a real-world dictionary where words (keys) have definitions (values). This foundation is crucial because dictionaries provide fast O(1) average time lookups, insertions, and deletions, making them efficient for many algorithms. Key concepts: 1. Creation: Use curly braces {} or dict() to create an empty dictionary. 2. Adding pairs: Assign a value to a key using dictionary[key] = value. If the key already exists, this updates its value. 3. Accessing values: Use dictionary[key] to get the value; if the key doesn't exist, this raises a KeyError. 4. Checking existence: Use 'key in dictionary' to check if a key exists without causing an error. 5. Keys must be immutable (e.g., numbers, strings, tuples), while values can be any type. Time complexity for basic operations is O(1) on average due to hashing. Space complexity depends on the number of key-value pairs stored. Connection to L4: This foundation enables the operations in L4, such as checking and modifying entries. Common mistakes include using mutable objects as keys (which is not allowed), confusing dictionaries with lists, or not handling missing keys properly. Real code pattern: # Create empty dict: my_dict = {} # Add key-value: my_dict['apple'] = 5 # Access: value = my_dict['apple'] # Check: if 'apple' in my_dict: print('Exists')

examples.input: Create dictionary, add key 'a' with value 1, then access it.
examples.trace: Step 1: Create empty dict: {}. Step 2: Add key 'a' with value 1: dict becomes {'a':1}. Step 3: Access value for key 'a': dict['a'] returns 1.

examples.input: Create dictionary with initial key 'x'=10, check if 'y' exists, then add 'y'=20.
examples.trace: Step 1: Create dict: {'x':10}. Step 2: Check if 'y' in dict -> no. Step 3: Add 'y' with value 20: dict becomes {'x':10, 'y':20}.

testCases.input: Create empty dictionary, add key 1 with value 100.
Create dictionary with key -5 and value -50.

approaches.name: Bitwise XOR O(n)
approaches.summary: Use XOR operation on all elements; duplicates cancel out, leaving the single number. This is optimal for this specific problem due to constant space and linear time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Single Number Using XOR
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find the element that appears once when all others appear twice using XOR operation.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the unique element in an array where every other element appears exactly twice. The XOR-based solution leverages the mathematical properties of the XOR operation: (1) a XOR a = 0 (any number XORed with itself cancels out to zero), (2) a XOR 0 = a (XORing with zero returns the original number), and (3) XOR is commutative and associative (order doesn't matter). The intuition is that when we XOR all elements together, pairs of identical numbers will cancel each other out (become 0), and the remaining XOR result will be the single number that doesn't have a pair. This works because XOR is both commutative (a XOR b = b XOR a) and associative ((a XOR b) XOR c = a XOR (b XOR c)), allowing us to process elements in any order. The algorithm is straightforward: initialize a result variable to 0, then iterate through each element in the array, XORing it with the result. After processing all elements, the result will be the single number. Time complexity is O(n) since we traverse the array once, and space complexity is O(1) as we only use a single variable. This approach is optimal for this specific problem because it meets the constraints with minimal resources. Common mistakes include not initializing the result to 0 (which would give incorrect results) or misunderstanding that XOR only works when duplicates appear exactly twice (it wouldn't work for thrice or other counts). The connection to L1 is that this full solution directly applies the XOR accumulation subroutine. Real code pattern: result = 0; for num in nums: result ^= num; return result.

examples.trace: Start with result = 0. Process 2: result = 0 XOR 2 = 2. Process 2: result = 2 XOR 2 = 0. Process 1: result = 0 XOR 1 = 1. Final result is 1.

Start with result = 0. Process 4: result = 0 XOR 4 = 4. Process 1: result = 4 XOR 1 = 5. Process 2: result = 5 XOR 2 = 7. Process 1: result = 7 XOR 1 = 6. Process 2: result = 6 XOR 2 = 4. Final result is 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — XOR Accumulation Over Array
ladders.desc: Implement a function that XORs all elements of an array together.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core computational step in the full problem: accumulating the XOR of all elements in an array. The intuition is that XOR, when applied sequentially across elements, combines them into a single result based on their binary representations. This works because XOR is a bitwise operation that toggles bits: if a bit is set in an element, XOR flips that bit in the result; if the same bit is set again (from a duplicate), it flips back, effectively canceling out. The algorithm initializes an accumulator variable to 0 (the identity element for XOR, since a XOR 0 = a). Then, it iterates through each element in the array, updating the accumulator by XORing it with the current element. After the loop, the accumulator holds the XOR of all elements. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing the array, and space complexity is O(1) using only the accumulator. This feeds into L0 as the essential operation that, when applied to an array with paired duplicates, yields the single number. Common mistakes include using an initial value other than 0 (which would skew results) or not handling empty arrays (though in the full problem, constraints ensure at least one element). Real code pattern: def xor_accumulate(arr): acc = 0; for x in arr: acc ^= x; return acc.

examples.trace: Start acc = 0. Process 1: acc = 0 XOR 1 = 1. Process 2: acc = 1 XOR 2 = 3. Process 3: acc = 3 XOR 3 = 0. Final acc is 0.
Start acc = 0. Process 5: acc = 0 XOR 5 = 5. Process 5: acc = 5 XOR 5 = 0. Process 5: acc = 0 XOR 5 = 5. Final acc is 5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — XOR Properties for Canceling Duplicates
ladders.desc: Understand how XOR's properties cause duplicate numbers to cancel out, leaving unique elements.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the mathematical insight that makes the XOR approach work: the properties of XOR that allow duplicates to cancel. The intuition is based on three key properties: (1) Identity: a XOR 0 = a, meaning XORing with zero leaves a number unchanged. (2) Self-inverse: a XOR a = 0, meaning XORing a number with itself results in zero. (3) Commutativity and associativity: a XOR b = b XOR a and (a XOR b) XOR c = a XOR (b XOR c), meaning the order of operations doesn't matter. When applied to an array with pairs of duplicates, these properties ensure that each pair XORs to 0 (due to self-inverse), and all these zeros XOR together (with any unique element) yield the unique element (due to identity). For example, in [x, x, y, y, z], computing XOR step-by-step: (x XOR x) = 0, then 0 XOR y = y, then y XOR y = 0, then 0 XOR z = z. The associative property allows us to group in any order, like (x XOR y XOR x XOR y XOR z) = z. Time and space are not directly applicable here as it's a conceptual level, but it underpins the O(n) time and O(1) space of L1. This feeds into L1 by explaining why XOR accumulation on such arrays gives the desired result. Common mistakes include assuming XOR works for any number of duplicates (it only works for even counts, specifically pairs) or not recognizing that XOR is bitwise, so it operates on binary bits. Real code pattern is conceptual, but can be demonstrated with small examples to show cancellation.

examples.trace: Consider array [3, 3, 5]. Compute XOR: 3 XOR 3 = 0 (duplicates cancel). Then 0 XOR 5 = 5 (identity property). So unique element 5 remains.
Array [2,2,4,4,6,6,8]. XOR stepwise: 2 XOR 2 = 0, 0 XOR 4 = 4, 4 XOR 4 = 0, 0 XOR 6 = 6, 6 XOR 6 = 0, 0 XOR 8 = 8. All pairs cancel to 0, leaving unique 8.

ladders.title: Building Block — Bitwise XOR Operation
ladders.desc: Learn how XOR works on binary numbers and its use in programming.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the XOR (exclusive OR) operation as a fundamental bitwise operator in programming. XOR compares corresponding bits of two numbers: if the bits are different, the result bit is 1; if they are the same, the result bit is 0. Intuitively, XOR acts as a 'toggle' or 'difference' operator: it flips bits where inputs differ. For example, in decimal, 5 (binary 101) XOR 3 (binary 011) = 6 (binary 110), because bits compare as: 1 vs 0 -> 1, 0 vs 1 -> 1, 1 vs 1 -> 0. In programming, XOR is often used for tasks like encryption, error detection, and, as in this problem, canceling duplicates due to its self-inverse property. The algorithm for computing XOR between two numbers is built into most languages via the '^' operator. Time complexity for a single XOR is O(1) as it operates on fixed-size integers (typically 32 or 64 bits), and space is O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the operational mechanism that enables the cancellation properties. Common mistakes include confusing XOR with logical OR (which doesn't cancel duplicates) or not understanding that XOR is bitwise, so it works on the binary representation, which is why it's efficient. Real code pattern: result = a ^ b; this computes the XOR of a and b.

examples.trace: 6 in binary: 110, 3 in binary: 011. XOR bit-by-bit: 1 vs 0 -> 1, 1 vs 1 -> 0, 0 vs 1 -> 1. Result binary: 101, which is decimal 5.
9 in binary: 1001, 9 in binary: 1001. XOR bit-by-bit: 1 vs 1 -> 0, 0 vs 0 -> 0, 0 vs 0 -> 0, 1 vs 1 -> 0. Result binary: 0000, which is decimal 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over an Array
ladders.desc: Master the skill of looping through an array to process each element.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental programming operation of iterating over an array (or list) to access and process each element sequentially. The intuition is that many algorithms, including the XOR accumulation, require examining every element in a collection to compute a result. Iteration allows us to apply an operation (like XOR) to each element in turn, building up a cumulative result. The algorithm typically uses a loop (e.g., for or while) that runs from the first index to the last, accessing elements via their indices or directly in languages like Python. For example, to sum an array, you'd initialize a sum to 0 and add each element during the loop. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the array length, as we visit each element once, and space complexity is O(1) if we only use a few variables. This feeds into L3 by providing the control structure needed to apply XOR across multiple numbers. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., missing the last element or going out of bounds) or not initializing the accumulator properly. Real code pattern: for element in array: # process element.

examples.trace: Start loop: index 0, element 1 -> print 1. Index 1, element 2 -> print 2. Index 2, element 3 -> print 3. Loop ends after last element.
Initialize sum = 0. Process 5: sum = 0 + 5 = 5. Process 10: sum = 5 + 10 = 15. Process 15: sum = 15 + 15 = 30. Final sum is 30.

testCases.input: Array [1, 1, 1], operation: check if all same
Array [0, 0, 0], operation: check if any non-zero

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Numbers and Bitwise Operations
ladders.desc: Understand binary representation and basic bitwise operators like AND, OR, XOR.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces binary numbers and bitwise operations, which are prerequisites for understanding XOR. Binary is a base-2 numeral system where numbers are represented using only 0s and 1s (bits). For example, decimal 5 is binary 101 (1*4 + 0*2 + 1*1). Bitwise operations perform logical operations on corresponding bits of numbers. The key operators are: AND (&) outputs 1 if both bits are 1; OR (|) outputs 1 if at least one bit is 1; XOR (^) outputs 1 if bits are different. The intuition is that computers use binary internally, so bitwise operations are fast and low-level. XOR, in particular, is 'exclusive OR' because it excludes the case where both bits are 1 (unlike OR, which includes it). This is why a XOR a = 0: all bits are the same, so each bit comparison yields 0. Time and space are not analyzed here as it's conceptual, but bitwise ops are O(1) per operation. This feeds into L4 by explaining the data representation used in iteration and XOR. Common mistakes include confusing bitwise XOR with logical XOR (which operates on boolean values) or not converting between decimal and binary correctly. Real code pattern: In Python, use bin() to see binary representation, and ^ for XOR.

examples.trace: Decimal 3: divide by 2: 3 / 2 = 1 remainder 1, 1 / 2 = 0 remainder 1. Read remainders backward: 11 in binary.
4 in binary: 100, 5 in binary: 101. AND bit-by-bit: 1 vs 1 -> 1, 0 vs 0 -> 0, 0 vs 1 -> 0. Result binary: 100, which is decimal 4.
